skill.name: gpt-image-edit
skill.description: Edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 (the `/edit` endpoint of ChatGPT Images 2.0) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents GPT Image Edit's strengths (preservation language, multilingual in-image text editing, multi-reference up to 10 images, layout / typography precision), the schema, and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / Flux Kontext / GPT Image 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "gpt image edit", "gpt-image-edit", "chatgpt image edit", "edit with gpt image 2", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images with GPT Image 2 on RunComfy, preserving identity through targeted edits and rewriting embedded text in multiple scripts.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate API wrapper skill for GPT Image 2 image editing on RunComfy. Static analysis flagged backtick syntax in markdown code blocks as shell execution patterns, but all detected patterns are documentation examples showing CLI command syntax. No actual command injection vectors exist. Network calls are to documented RunComfy API endpoints only. Credential storage uses standard secure file permissions (0600). No malicious patterns or user input exploitation found.

medium_findings.title: Backtick Syntax in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected backtick syntax across 59 locations in SKILL.md. These are markdown code block examples showing CLI commands (runcomfy run), not actual Ruby/shell execution. No command injection risk as these are documentation examples with hardcoded arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backticks appear in markdown fenced code blocks documenting CLI usage. The skill explicitly states 'The CLI does NOT shell-expand the prompt' (line 180), confirming no injection surface.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL References
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs in documentation (runcomfy.com, model-api.runcomfy.net). These are legitimate service endpoints documented for API usage, not data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are documented service endpoints. Security section (line 182) explicitly lists allowed outbound endpoints with no telemetry.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Placeholder Syntax
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged '<absolute/path>' in examples. This is documentation placeholder syntax, not actual path traversal vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill documentation uses angle-bracket placeholders for paths. The security section explains proper output-dir handling without user-controlled path manipulation.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access for Token Storage
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json access. This is legitimate secure credential storage with mode 0600 permissions, documented as a security feature.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Token storage uses standard secure patterns with owner-only permissions (0600). Environment variable override (RUNCOMFY_TOKEN) provides CI-safe alternative.

low_findings.title: MD5 Reference for File Checksums
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged MD5 mention. This refers to md5sum for file integrity verification in documentation, not actual cryptographic security use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: MD5 is mentioned in context of file checksum verification examples in documentation, not for cryptographic security purposes.

content.user_title: Edit Images with GPT Image 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Need to edit images while preserving identity, translating embedded text, or compositing multiple references? This skill provides Claude Code integration for GPT Image 2 edits with documented prompting patterns for precise, production-quality results.
actual_capabilities: Preserve identity through targeted edits (faces, poses, brand marks)
Rewrite embedded text in multiple scripts (Latin, kana, CJK, Cyrillic, Arabic)
Compose subjects from up to 10 reference images with directional language
Execute precise layout edits with spatial scopes (top-right to bottom-center)
Route to sibling models (Nano Banana, Flux Kontext) for batch or local edits
Output to local directory with automatic download from RunComfy CDN
limitations: Size accepts only 3 fixed values (1024x1024, 1024x1536, 1536x1024) plus auto
Long compound prompts cause drift; split into multiple passes for complex edits
Batch consistency for many SKU images better handled by Nano Banana Edit
Photorealism on portraits performs better with Nano Banana Pro

use_cases.title: Marketing Team Ad Localization
use_cases.description: Create multiple language variants of a single ad asset. Replace the in-image headline with localized text in Japanese kana, Cyrillic, or Arabic while preserving the photograph, layout, and brand mark exactly.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams managing global ad campaigns

use_cases.title: E-commerce Product Catalog Updates
use_cases.description: Swap headlines and CTAs across product images while maintaining identity. Move elements from top-right to bottom-center with directional language. Batch consistency benefits from routing to Nano Banana Edit for SKU galleries.

use_cases.title: Creative Studio Multi-Reference Compositing
use_cases.description: Compose a subject from one image into a room from another. Match lighting and color palette while preserving subject identity. Use numbered references (image 1, image 2) for correct cue routing.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the person's face, pose, and brand mark unchanged. Replace the background with a soft warm-grey studio sweep and a gentle floor shadow.

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual Text Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the photograph, layout, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads "[text]" in [script], same position and font weight as before.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reference Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Compose subject from image 1 into the room from image 2. Match the lighting and color palette of image 2. Keep image 1 subject identity (face, pose, clothing) unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Layout Repositioning
prompt_templates.prompt: Move the headline from top-right to bottom-center. Keep all other elements unchanged. Preserve the brand mark in the top-left corner.

output_examples.input: Keep the person in the photo, their pose, and clothing unchanged. Replace the background with a bright white-to-soft-gray studio sweep with gentle floor shadow.
output_examples.output: A professionally edited portrait with the same person, pose, and outfit against a clean studio background. The brand mark on their clothing remains intact while the background is cleanly replaced.

output_examples.input: Keep the photograph, layout, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the headline. The new headline reads "今日のおすすめ" in bold Japanese kana, same position and font weight.
output_examples.output: The original English headline is replaced with Japanese characters in the same position, size, and font weight. All other elements including the background scene, product photography, and brand colors are preserved.

best_practices: Lead with preservation goals. Always state what to keep unchanged before stating the change. The model honors preservation instructions stated up front.
Quote in-image text exactly when rewriting. Use the actual characters (e.g., "コーヒー") rather than paraphrasing ("Japanese word for coffee").
Use size: "auto" to preserve input aspect ratio unless the edit explicitly changes framing. Override only for intentional aspect ratio changes.
anti_patterns: Missing preservation language: Without explicit "keep X unchanged" instructions, the model may subtly rewrite faces, brands, or framing.
Long compound edit instructions: Asking to change A and B and C and D in one pass causes drift. Split into multiple passes for complex edits.
Paraphrasing in-image text: Writing "a Japanese coffee-related word" instead of quoting "コーヒー" results in different text output.

faq.question: How does this differ from GPT Image 2 text-to-image?
faq.answer: The edit endpoint modifies existing images while preserving specified elements. Text-to-image generates from scratch. Use edit for localization, repositioning, or composition. Use text-to-image for new asset generation.

faq.question: Can I edit multiple images in one request?
faq.answer: Yes. The images array accepts up to 10 publicly-fetchable HTTPS URLs. The first image is the primary edit target. Additional images serve as auxiliary cues for composition and style reference.

faq.question: What happens if my prompt is too complex?
faq.answer: Long compound edit instructions increase drift. Break complex edits into multiple sequential passes. Start with preservation-only, then layer in specific changes.

faq.question: How do I store my RunComfy API token securely?
faq.answer: Run 'runcomfy login' for browser-based device code authentication. This writes the token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 permissions. In CI environments, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.

faq.question: When should I use Nano Banana Edit instead?
faq.answer: Use Nano Banana Edit for batch consistency across many SKU images (up to 20 images per batch). It excels at maintaining consistent identity across product catalogs. Use GPT Image Edit for single high-precision edits with multilingual text.

faq.question: What size options are available?
faq.answer: Three fixed sizes: 1024x1024 (1:1), 1024x1536 (2:3 portrait), 1536x1024 (3:2 landscape), plus 'auto' which preserves the input aspect ratio. Any other value returns a 422 error.
